request.question: A Wish Way is a device that appears in a book illustrated by who?

title: The Fool of the World and the Flying Ship (book)
Wish Way
Tiger Tale
The Princess and the Pony
The Book of Genesis (comics)
The Lost King of Oz
Mother Goose in Prose
The Magic Finger
The Pocket Book of Boners
The Pencil of Nature
sentences: The Fool of the World and the Flying Ship is a book illustrated by Uri Shulevitz that retells a Russian fairy tale of the same name.
The text is taken from Arthur Ransome's version of the story in the 1916 book "Old Peter's Russian Tales"; Ransome had collected the folktale when he was a journalist in Russia.
The book was released in 1968 by Farrar, Straus and Giroux and won a Caldecott Medal for illustration in 1969.
A Wish Way is a device that appears in three of the Oz books of Ruth Plumly Thompson, "The Royal Book of Oz", "The Hungry Tiger of Oz", and "The Lost King of Oz".
Two Wish Ways are found in distinctly different locations, both in the Winkie Country.
Tiger Tale is a children's picture book illustrated by Marion Isham and written by Steve Isham.
First published in 2002, the book retells the Aboriginal story of how the Tasmanian tiger got its stripes.
"Tiger Tale" is illustrated using torn paper collage that gives the book a folkloric style.
The Princess and the Pony is a 2015 children's picture book illustrated by Kate Beaton.
She won the Children's Book Award in 2016 for the "Princess and the Pony".
She said that the book was inspired by her nephew.
It is her first book written for children.
The Book of Genesis (2009) is a comic book illustrated by American cartoonist and comic book artist Robert Crumb that purports to be a faithful, literal illustration of the Book of Genesis.
It reached #1 the New York Times graphic novel bestseller list and on the Christian books list at Amazon.com.
The Lost King of Oz (1925) is the nineteenth in the series of Oz books created by L. Frank Baum and his successors, and the fifth written by Ruth Plumly Thompson.
It was Illustrated by John R. Neill.
Mother Goose in Prose is a collection of twenty-two children's stories based on Mother Goose nursery rhymes.
It was the first children's book written by L. Frank Baum, and the first book illustrated by Maxfield Parrish.
It was originally published in 1897 by Way and Williams of Chicago, and re-released by the George M. Hill Company in 1901.
The Magic Finger is a fantasy story written by Roald Dahl in 1962.
It was first published in the United States, by Harper & Row in 1966, as a picture book illustrated by William Pène du Bois.
Allen & Unwin published a Pène du Bois edition in the U.K. in 1968.
Later editions have been illustrated by Pat Marriott, Tony Ross, and Quentin Blake.
The Pocket Book of Boners is a book illustrated by Theodor Seuss Geisel (Dr. Seuss), originally published as four separate books in 1931–32 by The Viking Press.
In 1941, Readers' League of America compiled these four books and published the "Pocket Book of Boners".
It was one of the bestselling paperback books of World War II, with 1.34 million copies in print by 1945.

The Pencil of Nature, published in six installments between 1844 and 1846, was the "first photographically illustrated book to be commercially published" or "the first commercially published book illustrated with photographs".
It was wholly executed by the new art of Photogenic Drawing, without any aid whatever from the

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: A Wish Way is a device that appears in a book illustrated by who?
hop1_docs: Wish Way | A Wish Way is a device that appears in three of the Oz books of Ruth Plumly Thompson, "The Royal Book of Oz", "The Hungry Tiger of Oz", and "The Lost King of Oz".  Two Wish Ways are found in distinctly different locations, both in the Winkie Country.

Felicity Wishes | Felicity Wishes is a British children's book series created, written, illustrated and privately owned by the Character Brand Creator Emma Thomson (not to be confused with the British actress of a similar name) .  Books include over 140 story books, activity books, picture books and annuals.  The popularity of the books led to the launch of a "Felicity Wishes" magazine and hundreds of other products.

I Wish That I Had Duck Feet | I Wish That I Had Duck Feet is a children's book written by Dr. Seuss, illustrated by B. Tobey, and first published in 1965.  "Theo.  LeSieg" was a pen name of Theodor Geisel, who is more commonly known as Dr. Seuss.  The story concerns a boy who wishes that he could have many different animal and mechanical body parts, explaining the pros and cons of the body part.  At the end, he decides that he is happiest being himself.

The Wishing Horse of Oz | The Wishing Horse of Oz (1935) is the twenty-ninth in the series of Oz books created by L. Frank Baum and his successors, and the fifteenth written by Ruth Plumly Thompson.  It was Illustrated by John R. Neill.  This book marked the point at which Thompson had written more Oz books than Baum himself.

Keeping the Night Watch | Keeping the Night Watch is a children's poetry book written by Hope Anita Smith and illustrated by E. B. Lewis.  Published by Square Fish Books as the sequel to Smith's "The Way a Door Closes", it was a Coretta Scott King Author Honor Book and appeared on several best children's book lists in 2009.

Jinnicky the Red Jinn | The Red Jinn, later known as Jinnicky, is one of Ruth Plumly Thompson's most frequently occurring characters in her Oz books.  He was first introduced in "Jack Pumpkinhead of Oz" as a mysterious figure who educates Jack Pumpkinhead on the use of the Pirate Sack.  Although a detailed description is included in the text, "Jack Pumpkinhead of Oz" includes no illustrations of the Red Jinn aside from a gruesome color plate that did not appear outside the first edition, in which he has massive piercings.  He was reintroduced, drawn, and given the name Jinnicky in "The Purple Prince of Oz"; he also appeared in the follow-up, "The Silver Princess in Oz".  (He makes a cameo appearance in "The Wishing Horse of Oz", and he is the principal pre-existing character in Thompson's sub-canonical penultimate Oz book, "Yankee in Oz").  Jack Snow apparently thought the Red Jinn was a separate character, for he considered "The Purple Prince of Oz" Jinnicky's first appearance in "Who's Who in Oz", though he did not include a separate Red Jinn entry.

A Lion in the Night | A Lion in the Night is a 1985 children's picture book written and illustrated by Pamela Allen.  It is about a baby who makes a wish, transforms her toy lion into a real one for one night, and goes on an adventure.
hop1_titles: I Wish That I Had Duck Feet
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - "A Wish Way is a device" (passage [1]) and it "appears in three of the Oz books of Ruth Plumly Thompson" listing "The Royal Book of Oz", "The Hungry Tiger of Oz", "The Lost King of Oz" (passage [1], quoted titles).
  - A separate Oz title shows an illustrator: "'Illustrated by John R. Neill.'" (passage [4]) — note this is for "The Wishing Horse of Oz", not one of the three listed in passage [1].
- Ambiguities:
  - Multiple candidate books are named in the passages: "The Royal Book of Oz", "The Hungry Tiger of Oz", and "The Lost King of Oz" (passage [1]). The question asks singularly which book's illustrator; the passages do not indicate which of these three is intended.
  - None of the three candidate book pages in the provided passages include their illustrators.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entities (exactly as in passages): "The Royal Book of Oz"; "The Hungry Tiger of Oz"; "The Lost King of Oz" (passage [1]).
  - Precise missing fact needed: the "Illustrated by" credit (the name of the illustrator) for whichever of these book pages the question intends (or for each, if ambiguous).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "The Royal Book of Oz" page — check the lede/infobox and any "Illustrated by" or publication/credits section for the illustrator name. (Search keywords: The Royal Book of Oz illustrator; check lede/infobox.)
  2) If needed, open "The Hungry Tiger of Oz" page — check lede/infobox/illustrations section for "Illustrated by". (Search: The Hungry Tiger of Oz illustrated by.)
  3) Open "The Lost King of Oz" page — check lede/infobox/illustrator/illustrations. (Search: The Lost King of Oz illustrator.)
  4) If the book pages are unclear, open "Ruth Plumly Thompson" bibliography or list of Oz books page to find illustrator credits for those titles; also check "John R. Neill" only if an illustrator name matches across titles.
Final answer deferred until those specific book pages/sections are retrieved and their "Illustrated by" credits verified.
response.hop2_query: The Royal Book of Oz illustrated by
hop2_docs: The Runaway in Oz | The Runaway in Oz is an Oz book by long-time Oz illustrator John R. Neill.  It was written originally during 1943 and was meant to be the thirty-seventh book in the Oz series.  However, Neill died before he could edit or illustrate the book.  Oz publisher Reilly & Lee decided not to publish the book due to shortages caused by World War II.  The text remained a possession of Neill's family.

Dorothy and the Wizard in Oz | Dorothy and the Wizard in Oz is the fourth book set in the Land of Oz written by L. Frank Baum and illustrated by John R. Neill.  It was published on June 18, 1908 and reunites Dorothy with the humbug Wizard from "The Wonderful Wizard of Oz" (1900).  This is one of only two of the original fourteen Oz books (the other being "The Emerald City of Oz" (1910), to be illustrated with watercolor paintings.

Lucky Bucky in Oz | Lucky Bucky in Oz (1942) is the thirty-sixth in the series of Oz books created by L. Frank Baum and his successors, and the third and last written and illustrated solely by John R. Neill.  (He wrote a fourth, "The Runaway in Oz", but died before illustrating it.)

Jack Pumpkinhead of Oz | Jack Pumpkinhead of Oz (1929) is the twenty-third of the series of Oz books created by L. Frank Baum and continued by other writers; it is the ninth Oz book written by Ruth Plumly Thompson.  It was Illustrated by John R. Neill.

Yankee in Oz | Yankee in Oz is a 1972 Oz novel by Ruth Plumly Thompson.  It was the first published by The International Wizard of Oz Club.  A letter from the Henry Regnery Company, which bought Reilly & Lee, is reproduced in the front indicating the publisher's blessing for the new Oz book to appear.  It was originally written in 1959, but because the Oz books were not selling, it was not published.  The first two editions of the book were published in 8½ x 11 inch format and running only 94 pages.  This was done at the request of illustrator Dick Martin to reduce the number of required illustrations and to show them closer to the actual size they were drawn.  The second printing (1986) featured a new cover, with the first edition artwork reprinted preceding the title page.  The third printing (2007) is standard Oz book size.  Its cover is a gaudier redesign of the second edition cover.  The book also features maps by James E. Haff, and as such, Thompson correctly places the Winkie Country in the west of Oz.

Kabumpo in Oz | Kabumpo in Oz (1922) is the sixteenth Oz book, and the second written by Ruth Plumly Thompson.  It was the first Oz book fully credited to her.  (Her first, "The Royal Book of Oz", was credited to L. Frank Baum on the cover.)  This is the last Oz book to enter in the public domain by means of automatic copyright expiration.  There are, however, other later Oz books in the public domain, due to their copyrights not being renewed.

The Wishing Horse of Oz | The Wishing Horse of Oz (1935) is the twenty-ninth in the series of Oz books created by L. Frank Baum and his successors, and the fifteenth written by Ruth Plumly Thompson.  It was Illustrated by John R. Neill.  This book marked the point at which Thompson had written more Oz books than Baum himself.
hop2_titles: Dorothy and the Wizard in Oz
response.summary_2: A Wish Way is a device that appears in three of Ruth Plumly Thompson's Oz books: The Royal Book of Oz, The Hungry Tiger of Oz, and The Lost King of Oz. The provided passages do not specify the "Illustrated by" credits for those three titles; they do show John R. Neill as a long-time Oz illustrator and as illustrator for Jack Pumpkinhead of Oz and The Wishing Horse of Oz.
